nodes.intent: Configuration has one source of truth: the repo. Every change flows through a branch, a PR, and a pipeline. Nothing is set manually.
Every resource type is denied until the platform has security controls and observability wired. Approval is the platform's commitment, not a task left to the application team.
Self-service, self-contained landing zones. The application team owns it end-to-end — what they build and how they run it is their call.
Platform-managed infrastructure carries no flat fees. Every service the platform provisions scales to zero when idle — fixed monthly costs are eliminated by design.

nodes.mechanism: Each application has a dedicated billing scope; every subscription it provisions bills to it exclusively.
nodes.why: Tags, filters, and manual splits are allocation logic — they drift. A dedicated billing scope makes cost accuracy structural, not maintained.

links.note: Application profile registration creates the invoice section.
Landing zone budget alerts are routed via the action group to the team that owns the spend.

nodes.mechanism: Only Azure Resource Manager (ARM) reachable services are eligible.
nodes.why: Without ARM reachability, the control plane has no reach — deny-by-default, declarative lifecycle, and diagnostic coverage all fail at once.

links.note: Self-service exemptions are viable because every resource is within Azure Policy's reach — a non-ARM service sits outside Policy's scope entirely.

nodes.mechanism: Every resource is denied by default. It appears on the allowed list only after the platform has approved it.
nodes.why: Without whitelisting, deny policies and diagnostic settings are assumed to be wired for every resource type — the approved list is what turns that assumption into a guarantee.

links.note: Must use an Allowed-prefixed assignment for resource-type compliance.
Every approved type must have a diagnostic settings definition.
New resource type triggers review of whether function-scoped roles need new actions.
Hard deny becomes a bottleneck without a self-service exemption path.
New resource type with no built-in Deny policy requires a custom definition before security coverage is complete.
ARM-reachability is the prerequisite for allowlisting — types outside ARM are ineligible.
Whitelisting triggers a Defender check — paid-SKU recommendations or remediation that requires cost go on the exemption list.

nodes.mechanism: When no built-in policy provides the required Deny or DeployIfNotExists effect, a custom definition is authored to close the gap.
nodes.why: Audit looks like coverage but the violation still occurs — a custom definition with the required effect is the difference between a guardrail that enforces and one that observes.

links.note: The naming convention constrains custom definitions to Deny, DeployIfNotExists, or Modify effects — the effect determines the assignment prefix.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy assignment names are [deny|config|allowed]-[Requirement].
nodes.why: Without a predictable pattern, teams can't find the right assignment without a portal lookup, and self-service breaks down.

links.note: Predictable names are what make exemptions self-service — teams can identify the correct assignment by name, no portal access required.

nodes.mechanism: Policy exemptions source assignment IDs from the generated reference file — never a hardcoded ARM ID.
nodes.why: Assignment ARM IDs change when a policy is redeployed; a hardcoded ID silently breaks every exemption that references it.

links.note: Stable, predictable names are the keys the reference file is indexed by — they don't change when a policy is redeployed.

nodes.mechanism: Every platform production deployment passes through a test environment first. This is enforced by branch protection rules and workflow structure.
nodes.why: Without an enforced gate, a single push can change production infrastructure with no prior validation.

links.note: Promotion gate targets oases-test for validation.

nodes.mechanism: Configuration has one committed source of truth — a single JSON file in the repo. Values are propagated to GitHub variables and Bicep parameters at deployment time. Nothing is set manually in the UI or injected out of band.
nodes.why: Without one committed source, environments drift. Onboarding a new engineer means tracing values through the GitHub UI instead of reading a file.

links.note: New repos receive a subset of platform variables at creation.
Per-environment client IDs sourced from this file.
githubVariables.json must be committed with initial platform config values before BigBang first runs.

nodes.mechanism: Deployment Stacks use deleteAll lifecycle. Any Azure resource no longer declared in the Bicep template is deleted on the next deployment. The template is source of truth — what's in the code is what exists in Azure.
nodes.why: deleteAll is what makes the template authoritative — without it, decommissioned subscriptions carry ghost resources, reuse is unsafe, and every teardown becomes a manual investigation.

links.note: deleteAll in test verifies code produces the expected Azure state before promotion to prod.
A subscription after deleteAll is guaranteed empty makes subscription reuse safe.
ARM-native resources are the only ones stack-managed azure-native-services-only is the prerequisite for deleteAll lifecycle enforcement.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy enforces hard denials across security disciplines on every resource type that supports each: public network access, local authentication, minimum TLS version, and cross-tenant replication.
nodes.why: Without enforcement, misconfiguration is invisible — nothing distinguishes a wrongly configured resource from an intentional one.

links.note: All disciplines deploy as deny- prefixed assignments — the prefix is what makes the enforcement category identifiable without a portal lookup.
Hard denial across disciplines is only sustainable if teams can unblock themselves — without it, operational pressure erodes each deny one at a time.
Where built-in policies provide only Audit effect for a discipline, a custom definition is required to close the enforcement gap.

nodes.mechanism: Gating and execution are separate workflow layers. Trigger workflows own conditions, branch rules, and environment gates. Template workflows own deployment logic. The two are never merged — a trigger calls a template, never the reverse.
nodes.why: Mixing gating with execution forces the same deploy logic to be duplicated per environment. A bug fix in deployment logic becomes a multi-file change.

links.note: Reusable workflows provide end-to-end deployment structure for each platform capability.
Gating lives in the trigger layer only.

nodes.mechanism: Each platform capability owns its Bicep modules and GitHub workflows end-to-end, so changes are fully isolated.
nodes.why: Without isolation, a change to one capability risks triggering or breaking another's deployment — the blast radius of every PR becomes unpredictable.

links.note: deleteAll on each capability's stack ensures no orphaned resources after a declaration is removed.
Main branch is protected by branch policies.
Capability configuration is sourced from the repo.
Each platform capability deploys to the test hierarchy before production.

nodes.mechanism: The platform has a one-time manual setup process to configure Azure management group and billing, Entra ID for authentication and GitHub organization.
nodes.why: BigBang cannot bootstrap its own prerequisites — it needs identity to authenticate, a hierarchy to deploy into, and a repo to store configuration.

links.note: App registration created, OIDC federation configured, and role assigned at the management group.
GitHub App created, installed in the org, and org-level secrets and variables seeded.
Subscription-bank management group needs to be created.
Gazelle-test and Gazelle-prod management groups need to be created.

nodes.mechanism: When a new landing zone is requested, the GitHub workflow queries Azure Resource Graph and finds the next free VNet address space. The address space is written to a landing zone parameter file.
nodes.why: Without an on-demand query, IPAM would need a dedicated registry to keep track of allocated addresses.

links.note: Address space is allocated without regional segmentation.

nodes.mechanism: Roles are scoped to a job function. A role describes what a person does (e.g., app engineer), not what resources they touch.
nodes.why: Resource-specific roles multiply with every newly whitelisted resource type, frequently requiring team members to apply for new roles.

links.note: The Application Profile creates an Entra ID group dedicated to application engineers, providing only the minimum access that does not break Infrastructure-as-Code principles.

nodes.mechanism: Platform-managed notifications are scoped to the action group and target the landing zone's engineers.
nodes.why: Hardcoded contacts go stale as teams change — alerts fire to former members, incidents go unanswered, and nobody notices until production is affected.

links.note: Contact details sourced from the Application Profile details.

nodes.mechanism: Registering an Application Profile is how an application joins the platform and gains the ability to provision its own landing zones.
nodes.why: Registration is the anchor — without it there is no billing scope for cost ownership, no contact details for notifications, and no repo for self-service. Everything downstream assumes it exists.

links.note: At landing zone creation, the subscription from the bank moves to this profile's invoice section.
The IDs created at registration (Entra group, invoice section) live as GitHub Variables on the application repo.
The GitHub App is used to create the application repo from the landingzone-template and seeds it with variables.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has automation runtime running inside its own subscription — job definitions and images are centrally managed by the platform, but execution happens locally.
nodes.why: Centralized automation would require a shared management subscription with baseline cost and team dependency.

links.note: Automation jobs query Entra ID, so landing zone identities receive Graph permissions.

nodes.mechanism: Every whitelisted resource type has diagnostic settings deployed automatically via DeployIfNotExists policy. Telemetry flows to the landing zone's Log Analytics workspace with no team action required.
nodes.why: Opt-in telemetry produces gaps that surface only during an incident.

links.note: Must use a Config-prefixed assignment for diagnostic settings.
Diagnostic settings target only Azure native resources.

nodes.mechanism: The platform provisions a delegated subnet for GitHub-hosted runners within the landing zone VNet — not a private endpoint.
nodes.why: Public network access is denied on every service — a public GitHub runner has no network path to the data plane.

links.note: Public runners have no data-plane path once public network access is denied.
Runners get a dedicated /28 subnet, delegated for runner integration, within the landing zone VNet.
GitHub App permissions are used for runner registration and configuration.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has a single managed identity that serves all its capabilities — GitHub workflows, automation jobs, and Entra ID queries.
nodes.why: Without a single identity, each capability needs its own RBAC and OIDC config — the permission surface multiplies with every new capability and auditing becomes impractical.

links.note: Automation jobs authenticate as the landing zone's managed identity.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zone provisioning draws from a subscription bank, returning the subscription to it when the landing zone is sunset.
nodes.why: MCA quota counts active and cancelled subscriptions equally. Always creating new subscriptions exhausts the quota ceiling — reuse is the only sustainable path.

links.note: Reused subscription must move to the application's invoice section before provisioning completes.

nodes.mechanism: The platform configures a Log Analytics workspace per landing zone, with retention set to stay within the free SKU.
nodes.why: A shared workspace merges ingestion costs across landing zones, which breaks the cost ownership model.

links.note: The cost of diagnostic ingestion lands on the application team's bill.
Diagnostic settings are platform-defined — the platform controls what ingests into this workspace
Log Analytics workspace is regional.

nodes.mechanism: Platform-managed landing zone resources live in a single resource group, protected by an Azure Deployment Stack.
nodes.why: A shared resource group blends platform and application resources. No clean boundary exists for teardown, RBAC, or incident management when both share the same scope.

links.note: Managed identity is provisioned inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack.
Resource group is the Deployment Stack scope — deleteAll tears down every platform-managed resource in one operation.
Log Analytics workspace lives inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack.
VNet is provisioned inside this resource group and managed by platform Deployment Stack
Container Apps environment and jobs are provisioned inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack

nodes.mechanism: The platform configures tags to identify application engineers and owners.
nodes.why: Manually applied tags drift within weeks — when an incident fires, the email points to someone who left the team, and the alert goes unanswered.

links.note: Nightly tag remediation runs as a Container App Job.
Engineer and owner emails are fetched from the application profile.
Tags are enforced via Azure Policy at the subscription level, using a Config-prefixed assignment name.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zones are stamped from a single shared template, but each is managed through its own dedicated parameter file and workflow.
nodes.why: Without per-zone parameter files and workflows, a team couldn't act on their landing zone without risking triggering another's redeployment.

links.note: Landing zone initial values come from the application profile.
Each landing zone has a dedicated workflow that passes parameters to the centralized template.
The platform identity is used to create and configure landing zone resources.
The landing zone identity receives Directory.Read.All at landing zone creation time.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone gets an isolated VNet, with no connectivity to other landing zones or on-premises networks.
nodes.why: A shared networking solution would introduce fixed costs, breaking the zero-cost-on-idle principle.

links.note: PaaS service firewalls must restrict public network access.
IPAM allocates the VNet's address space, defaulting to a /24.
Each VNet hosts its own runner subnet, registered with GitHub as a dedicated runner group.
The VNet is provisioned in AZURE_DEFAULT_LOCATION; a second region would require a new VNet and a separate regional IPAM allocation.

nodes.mechanism: The platform uses GitHub Container Registry as the shared image source for all automation jobs across all landing zones.
nodes.why: Azure Container Registry carries a flat monthly cost, breaking the zero-cost-on-idle guarantee.

links.note: GitHub Container Registry is a deliberate exception to azure-native-services-only — its cost profile outweighs the native-services preference here.

nodes.mechanism: A single Entra ID app registration serves as the platform identity, used to provision and configure both the platform and its landing zones.
nodes.why: A managed identity requires an Azure resource as its host, but the platform has no subscription of its own — an app registration exists in Entra ID independently of any Azure infrastructure.

links.note: The platform identity holds Directory.ReadWrite.All and Application.ReadWrite.All, used to perform appRoleAssignedTo during landing zone provisioning.

nodes.mechanism: All platform and landing zone workflows authenticate to GitHub using a single, shared GitHub App identity.
nodes.why: Have not found a solution to create a GitHub App per application/environment.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zone-managed identities receive Microsoft Graph permissions, allowing them to query Entra ID objects.
nodes.why: Without these permissions, landing zone automation can't detect Entra ID users, groups and applications.

links.note: Receives Directory.Read.All at landing zone creation time.
Graph permissions are granted via admin consent as a manual prerequisite.

nodes.mechanism: Platform changes deploy to a prod-identical test environment.
nodes.why: Without a parallel hierarchy, platform changes would hit production directly, with failures landing on live landing zones.

links.note: Template workflow changes are validated here first.
The 'Application Registration' workflow is validated here before any platform change reaches production.
New whitelisted resource types are manually tested here before reaching production.
Test has its own dedicated app registration, sourced from a GitHub variable.
Test identity holds the same Graph permissions as production.
Test and production share the same GitHub App.

nodes.mechanism: The platform provides a set of Defender for Cloud exemptions, covering recommendations where remediation would require a paid SKU or other fixed-cost infrastructure.
nodes.why: Recommendations with no free remediation path, left unexempted, inflate the compliance backlog with items nobody can act on — the score becomes noise.

links.note: Defender exemptions follow the same long-lived exemption mechanism — the PR is the review and audit record.
The cost of a Defender for Cloud paid SKU lands on the application's subscription billing.

nodes.mechanism: Application teams can request a temporary or long-lived exemption from a specific Azure Policy assignment via a self-service workflow.
nodes.why: The hard deny is only sustainable if teams can unblock themselves — without that, deny-by-default erodes under operational pressure.

links.note: Exemptions reference a JSON file to look up the policy assignment ID — never a hardcoded ARM ID.

nodes.mechanism: A single region keeps the availability model, network topology, deployments, and service parity consistent across all landing zones.
nodes.why: Without that constraint, every deployment would carry its own region decision, and service parity would depend on each engineer's choice rather than being a platform guarantee.

links.note: The region is defined once in githubVariables.json — all deployments read it from there.
GitHub runner VNet integration is region-gated, so the platform's chosen region must support it.
Application profile registration creates the invoice section.
Landing zone budget alerts are routed via the action group to the team that owns the spend.
Self-service exemptions are viable because every resource is within Azure Policy's reach — a non-ARM service sits outside Policy's scope entirely.

Where built-in policies provide only Audit effect for a discipline, a custom definition is required to close the enforcement gap.

Gating lives in the trigger layer only.

operations.intent: A new recurring job is added to the platform automation.
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[] to understand access, identity, and image constraints before writing any code.
Implement the job logic under landing-zones/automation/ — jobs must use the ARM API only; no data-plane or VNet-dependent calls.
Add a Dockerfile for the new image — base image must be pullable from a public registry without authentication.
Register the new job in landing-zones/bicep/modules/landingzone-automation.bicep following the existing job definition pattern.
Verify the job authenticates as the landing zone identity — no separate identity or secret.
Present a complete draft of all changes before implementing.

operations.intent: A resource type is admitted to the platform only after security controls, diagnostic settings, and access roles are fully wired.
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[].
Add the resource type to allowedResources.json.
Use mcp__azure__documentation microsoft_docs_search to find the built-in diagnostic settings policy — add it to diagnosticSettings.bicepparam.
For each file in oases/, use mcp__azure__documentation microsoft_docs_search to find built-in policies with the required effect — add matching entries.
For each gap where no built-in policy provides the required effect, author a custom definition in customDefinitions/ and register it in policyDefinitions.bicepparam.
Check defenderForCloudExemptions.jsonc for paid-SKU recommendations specific to this resource type — add qualifying entries.
Check accessControl.bicepparam for custom role actions needed by this resource type.
Present a complete draft of all changes before implementing.

operations.intent: A new isolated Azure environment is provisioned under the application team's billing scope, with guardrails pre-wired and ready for workload deployment.
steps: Confirm the application profile exists under application-profiles/ — if not, the register-application-profile operation must be completed first.
Collect from the user: application name, environment (test or prod), management group name (oases-prod or oases-test), and budget (optional, default 100).
Auto-select the subscription ID: run `az account management-group subscription show-sub-under-mg --name subscription-bank --query "[0].name" -o tsv`.

Confirm the values with the user (including the auto-selected subscription ID), then trigger the workflow: gh workflow run requestNew-Landing-Zone.yml -f ApplicationName="{AppName}" -f Environment="{env}" -f ManagementGroupName="{mgmtGroup}" -f SubscriptionId="{subscriptionId}" -f Budget="{budget}" — this raises a pull request.
A platform engineer reviews and approves the pull request — approval is what triggers the automation to complete provisioning.

operations.intent: A landing zone is permanently torn down, all resources destroyed, and the subscription returned to the subscription-bank — no manual cleanup left behind.
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[] to understand what is being destroyed and what constraints apply.
Collect from the user: subscription ID of the landing zone to decommission.
Verify the bicepparam file exists at landing-zones/oases-prod/{app}/{env}.bicepparam and the lz workflow at .github/workflows/lz-oasis-{appName}-{env}.yml — do not proceed if either is missing.

Present to the user what will be permanently destroyed: deployment stack, resource groups, role assignments, budget, Defender settings. Confirm the subscription returns to the bank. Wait for explicit confirmation.

Trigger lz-flow-destroy-landing-zone via workflow_dispatch with the subscription ID. Do not manually delete files — the workflow resolves app name and env, creates the cleanup PR, and the merge triggers the Azure destroy sequence.

operations.intent: An application team is registered with the platform, giving them a billing scope and a GitHub repo — the prerequisite for full self-service control over their landing zones.
steps: Collect from the user: application name, owner email, and engineer email.

Confirm the values with the user, then trigger the workflow: gh workflow run requestNew-Application-Profile.yml -f ApplicationName="{AppName}" -f OwnerContact="{ownerEmail}" -f engineerContact="{engineerEmail}" — this raises a pull request.
A platform engineer reviews and approves the pull request — approval is what triggers the automation to complete provisioning.

operations.intent: The knowledge graph remains accurate, consistent, and self-contained — every entry follows the schema, links are organic and directional, and the validation script passes before any PR is opened.
steps: Identify whether this is a new entry or an update to an existing one. For an update, read the existing file first.
Determine the type: guiding-principles, decisions, or operations.

Draft the entry. For decisions: id matches filename; decision is a single clear statement; why explains the consequence of not following it; links are organic and unidirectional — each note answers 'how does this linked decision specifically affect or constrain this decision?'; violations are specific enough to detect in a code review; files list only files where a reviewer would look to verify the decision is respected.
For links: only add one if the note would change how someone implements or reviews this decision. Drop it if the relationship is obvious from context alone.
Present the complete draft to the user before writing any file.
Write the file.
Run the validation script: bash .claude/validate-knowledge-base.sh. Fix any errors before proceeding.
Open a PR.
violations: writing a file before presenting a draft to the user
link note that does not answer 'how does this linked decision specifically affect or constrain this decision?'
violation that restates the decision instead of describing a detectable breach

operations.intent: An existing landing zone is updated — cost ceiling, tags, or policy exemptions — entirely through the repo, with no portal access required.
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[] to understand the constraints on tags, budget, and exemptions before proposing any change.

Verify the landing zone exists: the bicepparam file at landing-zones/oases-{env}/oases-{appName}-{env}.bicepparam and the workflow at .github/workflows/lz-oasis-{appName}-{env}.yml — do not proceed if either is missing, this is a create-landing-zone task.
Collect from the user the app/env to manage and which concern to change: cost, tags, or exemptions.
Cost: edit the single `budget` value only. Do not add actual-spend or additional thresholds — the budget drives a single Forecasted alert.

Tags: edit `subscriptionLevelTags` / `resourceLevelTags` keys and placement only. Keep ownerEmail and engineerEmail as readEnvironmentVariable references, never hardcoded values (landing-zone-tags, application-profile); keep ownerEmail at subscription level, never resource level.

Exemptions: ask the user whether the exemption is temporary or long-lived. Temporary -> trigger lz-flow-create-policy-exemption (8-hour expiry, break-glass, no PR record). Long-lived -> add an entry to the `exemptions` array referencing a policy-assignment-reference.json key, never a hardcoded ARM assignment id (self-service-policy-exemptions, azure-policy-reference). defenderRecommendationExemptions and diagSettingsExemption remain platform-managed booleans.
Present a complete draft of the bicepparam change before implementing.

For declarative changes (cost, tags, long-lived exemptions) open a PR; the merge triggers lz-oasis-{appName}-{env}.yml, redeploying the landing zone stack with deleteAll. No portal or out-of-band edits (end-to-end-deployment).
